coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive detection of glyphosate through effective photoelectron transfer between CdTe and chitosan derived carbon dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Bera, Mihir K.
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: The rapid on-filed detection of organophosphorus herbicide residue is a challenge for environmental protection as well as food safety. In this connection we have developed a CdTe-CQD integrated probe for the detection of herbicide glyphosate in aqueous medium. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of CdTe-CQD pair shows diminished fluorescence at 619 nm due to efficient photoelectron transfer from CdTe to CQD. When glyphosate is added into CdTe-CQD at pH 8, the disintegration of pair takes place hence which inhibits the PET process and regains the emission intensity of the probe at 619 nm. This sensing protocol works well in real samples. The fluorescence assay for glyphosate (F/F0) covers a linear concentration range from 0 to 1000 nM and the detection limit is found to be 2 pM. Compared to previously reported quantum dot and metal nanoparticle based sensing methods our sensor is simple and works for a broad linear range with high sensitivity. The specific and selective recognition of our probe towards glyphosate even in presence of other organophosphorus pesticides makes it suitable for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot (CQD)
CdTe
PET
Turn-on sensing
Glyphosate

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from chitosan
Synthesis and characterization of CQD-CdTe nanoprobe
Optical properties of chitosan derived CQD-CdTe nanoprobe
PL response of CQD-CdTe probe towards glyphosate
Determination of glyphosate in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by DST SERB Govt. of India (CRG/2018/001717). MKB thanks to NIT Rourkela for providing fellowship.
This work is financially supported by DST SERB Govt. of India ( CRG/2018/001717 ). MKB thanks to NIT Rourkela for providing fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV spectra of chitosan and CQD derived from chitosan, (b) PL spectra of CQD derived from chitosan, (c) UV spectra of CdTe, (d) PL of CdTe quantum dots.
Fluorescence spectrum of CdTe (50 μL) in presence of increasing concentration of CQD solution.
(a) TEM image of CQD@CdTe solution showing close association of both QD, (b) High resolution TEM showing lattice fringe corresponding to [101] and [111] plane of CdTe and CQD.
(a,b) Fluorescence response of CQD@CdTe solution, (c) Linear range, (d) Change in FL life time of CQD-CdTe probe after adding glyphosate.
(a) Effect of pH on sensing, (b) time on sensing.
Comparison of selectivity of CQD-CdTe nanoprobe towards glyphosate when compared to other possible interfering species.
Illustration of sensing mechanism of CQD-CdTe chemosensor towards glyphosate.
Comparison of linear range and limit of detection of glyphosate by different reported spectrophotometric methods.
Analytical performance of CQD@CdTe probe towards glyphosate in real agricultural samples.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive detection of glyphosate through effective photoelectron transfer between CdTe and chitosan derived carbon dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, 769008, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, India-769008

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid on-filed detection of organophosphorus herbicide residue is a challenge for environmental protection as well as food safety. In this connection we have developed a CdTe-CQD integrated probe for the detection of herbicide glyphosate in aqueous medium. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of CdTe-CQD pair shows diminished fluorescence at 619 nm due to efficient photoelectron transfer from CdTe to CQD. When glyphosate is added into CdTe-CQD at pH 8, the disintegration of pair takes place hence which inhibits the PET process and regains the emission intensity of the probe at 619 nm. This sensing protocol works well in real samples. The fluorescence assay for glyphosate (F/F0) covers a linear concentration range from 0 to 1000 nM and the detection limit is found to be 2 pM. Compared to previously reported quantum dot and metal nanoparticle based sensing methods our sensor is simple and works for a broad linear range with high sensitivity. The specific and selective recognition of our probe towards glyphosate even in presence of other organophosphorus pesticides makes it suitable for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: During the last century, pesticides and herbicides have become an important component of agricultural system contributing towards a noticeable increase in crop yields and food production. Specifically organophosphates are used worldwide to control a wide variety of pests to enhance the quality as well as quantity of agricultural products. Glyphosate (GP) (N-Phosphomethylglycine) is a systemic herbicide used against crops which are genetically modified to increase their tolerance [1–4]. Glyphosate has become one of the most widely used herbicide worldwide because of its outstanding weed control capability and relatively low harmfulness to living organism. However, due to high solubility in water and leakage, its residue accumulates both in soil and water. Such residue causes serious detrimental effects on central nervous system due to irreversible inhibition effect on acetylcholinesterase. The average half-life period of glyphosate in water varies from a few days to 91 days [5]. As per to the US Environmental protection Agency (EPA), the maximum tolerance limit of glyphosate in drinking water is 0.7 ppm [6]. In addition to its inactivating effect on acetylcholinesterase leading to neurotoxicity, a recent study indicates that GP affects cell cycle regulation and also inhibits the secretion of steroid hormones in men which create infertility, adverse effects to animals, aquatic vegetation and proliferation of breast cancer in humans [7,8]. Therefore, developing effective technologies to detect toxic GP is highly desirable. For successful quantitative determination of glyphosate a few analytical methods have been reported in literature which includes HPLC, ion exchange chromatography [9], capillary electrophoresis [10], mass spectrometry [11], gas chromatography [12], ion chromatography [13], colorimetric [14] and even enzyme- linked immune sorbent assay [15]. In spite of reasonable performance, most of them are expensive and require sophisticated instrumentation along with skilled technicians.

Detection of toxic analyte through fluorescence spectroscopy has been emerged as an effective method owing to its high sensitivity, easy operational protocol and affordability. Innovative surface derivatization techniques on quantum dot may offer cost-effective fluorescence detection method for sensing pollutant molecules and microbes [16–19]. Guo et al. have designed a fluorescence assay for the detection glyphosate where the emission intensity of CdTe quantum dot was reduced due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between CdTe and gold nanoparticles facilitated by electrostatic interaction [20]. The excellent fluorescence property and efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) could bring down the detection limit of such sensor to 9.8 ng/kg. Pletschke et al. have reported nanofiber based colorimetric sensor strip made up of copper doped poly (vinyl) alcohol sensor strip for sensing glyphosate and its structural analogues. The sensor strip validated a practical detection limit of 0.1 μg/mL and a rapid response time of 1–3 s [21]. In recent times, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging as promising fluorophores in imaging and sensing applications of a wide variety of biological and environmental analytes owing to their easy synthesis, high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY), tunable fluorescence property and high photostability [22]. The brilliant fluorescence property of CQD has been well explored in the detection of a few number of pesticides such as atrazine [23], simapine [24], carbaryl [25], paraxon [26] and glyphosate [27–31]. To the best of our knowledge till date there are only limited papers reported on CQD fluorescence sensor for the detection of very important herbicide like glyphosate. For instance, Ding et al. have prepared CQD from microwave irradiation of thiourea and ethylene glycol [27]. The reduced fluorescence of the CQD-Cu2+ complexed can be recoverable on addition of glyphosate. The sensing strategy is based on a competitive affinity for Cu2+ between glyphosate and the surface functional groups of CQD, which offers the fluorescence recovery of CD-Cu2+ complex. Yu et al. have developed a highly sensitive detection method for glyphosate based antigen-antibody reaction strategy of carbon dot labelled antibody and antigen-magnetic beads [28]. Hu et al. have developed CQD from hydrothermal decomposition of citric acid and Tris. The intense blue emission of CQD was effectively quenched by the presence of glyphosate based on FRET between CD and glyphosate [29]. Lin et al. have developed CQD-Au NP FRET sensor for organophosphorus pesticides where the emission intensity of CQD was remarkably quenched in the presence of Au NPs as a result of effective FRET between CQD and Au NP driven by electrostatic interaction. The quenched fluorescence was recovered on addition of acetylthiocholine (ATC) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) [30]. Ruiz-Medina et al. have reported GQD-Ag NP for sensitive detection of glyphosate in food samples [31]. Although these carbon quantum dot based protocols can be successfully used for fluorescence detection of glyphosate, however the use of expensive biomolecules and Au in the sensor, makes the process costly and uncertain for large scale practical application. Therefore, while designing fluorescence sensors for glyphosate proper attention should be focused on well-defined sensing mechanism, reproducible performance in real samples and most importantly cost-effectiveness of the sensing protocol.

In comparison to other complicated and expensive fluorescence detection methods, photo electron transfer (PET) strategy which is based on long range dipolar interactions through excited donor and acceptor is simple and practical. PET sensor offers high accuracy over the single wavelength emission probe by eliminating the interference of background signals. In this work, we have integrated mercaptopropionic acid capped CdTe and carbon quantum dot (CQD) synthesized from chitosan to construct a fluorescence probe where the emission of CdTe is completely quenched in the presence of CQD. However in presence of glyphosate the fluorescence of CdTe turns on because of disintegration of CdTe and CQD.

Glyphosate, tellurium powder and mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Cadmium chloride, and glacial acetic were obtained from Merck Chemical. Sodium borohydride was purchased from SRL Chemical, India and Chitosan was obtained from Himedia Chemical. Dichloromethane was dried over P2O5.

CdTe was prepared following a procedure reported by wang et al. [32]. 63 mg of tellurium powder and 100 mg of NaBH4 were taken in a 50 mL round bottle flask. After addition of 3 mL of ultrapure water in an inert atmosphere, a black colloid was formed which was further stirred for 10 h yielding a purple NaHTe solution. The reaction mixture was kept at 4 °C in the dark and was used in the next step. The supernatant NaHTe was used as precursor for the synthesis of CdTe QDs. 230 mg of CdCl2, 2.5 H2O and 210 μL of MPA were dissolved in 125 mL of ultrapure water and the pH of this solution was adjusted to 11 using 1.0 M NaOH followed by continuous stirring for 30 min under inert atmosphere. A freshly prepared NaHTe solution as was added to the above solution under nitrogen atmosphere. After addition of NaHTe, the colour of the mixture was turned from colourless to orange. The mixture was refluxed for 48 h at 80 °C and red-emissive CdTe QDs were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dot was prepared from hydrothermal treatment of Chitosan (Degree of deacetylation 75 %, Mol. Wt. 3500−20000 Da) following a reported procedure [33]. A solution of chitosan, 2 gm completely dissolved in 18 mL of 2% acetic acid, was subjected to hydrothermal treatment in an autoclave (Berghof BT300) at 180 °C for 12 h. After being cooled naturally to room temperature, the orange colour suspension was filtered with a syringe filter (0.22 μm) to take out the larger residues. The filtrate thus obtained was dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut off 1000 Da. Finally, an orange colour aqueous dispersion containing CQD was obtained from which CQD was recovered by lyophilisation.

100 μL of CQD solution (0.1 mg/mL), 3.81 mL H2O and 50 μL CdTe mixed and incubated for 60 min. 40 μL of glyphosate solution with variable concentration was added to it and kept for 30 min. The PL spectrum has been recorded at λex = 360 nm and λem=640 nm. Fluorescence response of the probe towards some expected interfering pesticides such as quinalphos, atrazine, imidaclopid, chloropyrifos and cation NH4 +, Na+ etc were also verified at similar experimental conditions. For solubilizing quinalphos, atrazine, imidaclopid, chloropyrifos a mixture of ethanol and water (1:3) has been used as solvent.

Horiba FluoroMax-4 fluorimeter spectrophotometer was used to collect emission spectrum in all sensing experiments. In order to investigate morphology, microstructure and composition of the CQD-CdTe fluorophore a HRTEM microscope (FEI Technai G2 30ST) operated at 300 kV was used. IRAffinity-1S, Shimadzu, a compact FTIR spectrophotometer was used to identify the surface functional groups.

Cucumber, capsicum and ginger are comestible vegetable those were bought from local market and treated with three different concentrations of glyphosate solutions. For each vegetable sample the concentration calibration curve has been plotted using known concentration of glyphosate solution in the same non spiked sample extract. Then the spiked vegetables were ground and juices were extracted. These juices were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm ad the supernatant was subjected to fluorescence assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQD-CdTe nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD were synthesized by carbonization of polymerized precursor followed by self-surface passivation process. The polymerization is likely via formation of radical cations and radical coupling (condensation and intra-molecular cyclization), leading to the formation of CQD dots self-functionalized with amino/carboxyl on their surfaces. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the chitosan-derived CQD shows similar characteristics. In comparison to the corresponding starting material chitosan, CQD shows absorption band at 283 nm. These red-shifts in the UV absorption spectrum reveal smaller electronic band gaps in CQDs when compared to chitosan (Fig. 1 a). The comparison of FTIR spectrum of chitosan with that of chitosan derived CQD indicates formation of CQD. In case of chitosan there is a broad peak associated with OH stretching and NH stretching from 3008 to 3032 cm−1, CH stretching vibrations at 2924 and 2873 cm−1, NH bending vibrations at 1652 and 1550 cm−1 and CH as well as NH bending vibrations of the pyranose ring at 1160–990 cm−1 [34,35]. For carbon dots the peak at 1640 cm−1 becomes very strong because of stretching vibration of CC bond. Apart from that a remarkable change in peak pattern can be observed in the C–H bending region (Fig. S1). The TEM image shows that the diameter of CQDs is within 3−6 nm (Fig. S2). The high resolution TEM image shows lattice imaging of [101] plane of graphite having d value 0.29 nm. CdTe quantum dots were synthesized in aqueous phase using tellurium powder, sodium borohydride and cadmium chloride as chemical precursors. The surface of the CdTe was passivated by using mercaptopropionic acid. With increase in reaction time, UV/Vis absorption spectra of CdTe show a red-shift from 460 nm to 611 nm and confirm the particle synthesis (Fig. 1c). This shift of absorption maxima to higher wavelength demonstrate the lowering of electronic band gap with rise in reaction time which is attributed to the increase in particle size with progress of the reaction. The particle size was calculated from UV absorption data following the method reported by Yu et al. [36] and shown in Fig. 1c. Further, X-ray diffractogram shows peaks at 2θ = 25.24, 39.84 and 45.079 which corresponds to the planes [111], [220] and [311] of CdTe (Fig. S3). Such diffraction pattern indicates CdTe have a zinc blende crystal configuration (JCPDS 752086). TEM image of CdTe indicates that the diameters of particles are mainly in the range of 3−5 nm (Fig. S4). FTIR of CdTe QDs exhibit four distinct peaks at 3360, 2915, 1560, 1390 and 915 cm−1 corresponding to –O-H stretching of surface −COOH, -C–H stretching−COO- and –C–H bending vibrations respectively. The disappearance of –SH stretching bands at 2550 cm−1 indicates the ligation MPA on the surface of CdTe through –SH group (Fig. S5). The TEM image displays uniform distribution of CQD-CdTe nanoprobe within a size range of 2−10 nm. The high resolution TEM image exhibits a lattice spacing of 0.28 nm and 0.38 nm which matches with the [200] plane of graphite and [101] plane of CdTe respectively. The STEM-EDAX indicates that the constructed nanoprobe is mainly composed of C, N, O, Cd and Te with atomic weight % of 98.06, 0.44, 1.47, 0.02 and 0.01 respectively. The EDS mapping of CQD-CdTe QDs shows uniform distribution of each element in our as-synthesized nanoprobe (Fig. 3c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of chitosan derived CQD-CdTe nanoprobe
PL response of CQD-CdTe probe towards glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of change in pH on the intensity enhancement of the emission band was evaluated with a fixed glyphosate concentration, in order to find out the operational pH range of the sensor. When pH of the medium was 8, the maximum increase in (F/F0) towards glyphosate (Fig. 5 a) was achieved. Fluorescence intensity vs time plot was drawn at a fixed concentration 100 nM to find out the minimum response time of the sensing system. The fluorescence intensity attained maximum value after 20 min and remains constant after that. Incubation for a longer period did not result any decay in fluorescence signal.

When glyphosate is added to CQD-CdTe pair the emission intensity at 632 nm enhances because of perturbation of photo induced electron transfer from CdTe to CQD. In between pH 6.3–11.0 glyphosate exists in the zwitter ionic form (Scheme 1 ). As glyphosate is added to the mixed solution of CQD-CdTe, the positively charged N interacts with surface carboxylate anion of MPA coated CdTe, thus separating CdTe-CQD pair and hence the PET is restricted causing a significant increase in the intensity at 632 nm. Therefore a very sensitive and superior response has been attained even at picomolar concentration. The proposed mechanism is also supported by hydrodynamic size measurement (Fig. S7). It is found that when the GP concentration varied from 0 to 400 nM, DLS size of CQD-CdTe pair was reduced 480 to 300 nm. In addition to peaks at lower region also appeared at higher glyphosate concentration attributed to the presence of individual CQD and/CdTe quantum dots. Fluorescence life time measurements give insight to our proposed mechanism. To our expectation, the fluorescence lifetime of the sensor was increased from 76.37 ns to 93.21 ns after addition of glyphosate (Fig. 4d). To further authenticate our proposed mechanism we did one control experiment. When glycine or alanine was added to the CdTe-CQD pair instead of GP, there was no such regain of fluorescence signal at pH 8 (Figs. S8–S10), indicating that arrangement of CQD-CdTe pair is not disturbed by alanine or glycine. Compared to the structure of the glyphosate at pH 8 no such positively charged nitrogen is present in glycine or alanine, hence the distribution of CQD-CdTe pair is not affected by the presence of these molecules.

The selective recognition of our probe towards glyphosate was examined in presence of different pesticides and other interfering anions. When compared with the fluorescence enhancement effect from pesticides such as quinalphos, imidacloprid, cypermethirin and chlorpyrifos, our nanoprobe shows significantly high turn on effect towards glyphosate. The FL intensity enhancement observed in case of glyphosate is almost 6 times higher than other pesticides (Fig. 6 ). The effects of two commonly used agricultural ions (ammonium hydrogen phosphate and sodium hydrohen phosphate) shows no such impact on our sensor system. Highest selectivity of CQD-CdTe probe towards glyphosate over other pesticides can be ascribed to the electrostatic interaction of surface carboxylates of CdTe and zwitter ionic form of glyphosate at pH 8 which does not exist in case of other pesticides (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of this sensor has been validated by carrying out the sensing experiment in three different vegetable samples. Cucumber, capsicum and ginger have been incubated with known concentration of glyphosate solutions. For each sample the corresponding non-spiked sample was used as references. The calibration curve against each sample was plotted using standard glyphosate solution in the same sample extract. The recovery percentage varies from 90 to 110which shows that this fluorescence sensor system has reasonable accuracy and can be utilized for the detection of glyphosate in practical samples (Table 2 ).

The work described in this paper has established a selective and ultrasensitive fluorimetric assay for the detection of organophosphorus herbicide glyphosate based on efficient electron transfer between oppositely charged CQD and CdTe. MPA modified CdTe and chitosan derived carbon dot can make electron donor-acceptor assembly which effectively reduce fluorescence signal of CdTe. In the presence of zwitter ionic glyphosate the assemblage between CdTe and CQD is lost and electron transfer is attenuated. In optimum condition the fluorescence response is linearly propotional to the concentration of glyphosate in the range of 0–1000 nM and the limit of detection was found to be 2 pM. In comparison to the reported organic fluorophore and precious metal nanoparticle based sensing method our method offers a cost effective, sensitive and reliable protocol which works for broad linear range. Due to simple operational protocol and reproducibility this method would possess promising applications in agricultural and environmental fields.
Mihir K. Bera has synthesized, characterized and performed all sensing experiments under the supervision of Dr. Sasmita Mohapatra.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by DST SERB Govt. of India (CRG/2018/001717). MKB thanks to NIT Rourkela for providing fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.124710.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
